Country.summary: Story-driven songs built on simple harmony, open chords, and a strong backbeat or shuffle. Nashville and outlaw traditions share the same I–IV–V backbone but differ in production and lyric tone.
tropes: Three- and four-chord loops in major keys
Mixolydian colour on the V chord (dominant 7th)
Pedal steel, fiddle, and telecaster twang
Lyrics about roads, heartbreak, small towns, and work

progressions.name: Honky-tonk backbone
progressions.notes: The default country turnaround; add 7ths on IV and V for extra grease.

progressions.name: Nashville pop lift
progressions.notes: Borrowed from pop; huge in modern country radio.

progressions.name: Two-step waltz
progressions.notes: 3/4 or 6/8 feel; common in ballads and dance-floor waltzes.

Country.rhythm: Straight 4/4 or shuffle 12/8; train-beat snare on 2 and 4
Country.instruments: Acoustic guitar, telecaster, pedal steel, fiddle, upright or electric bass

Bluegrass.summary: High-energy acoustic music from Appalachia. Fast picking, close vocal harmony, and chord changes that move quickly but stay diatonic.
tropes: Flatpicking and crosspicking on acoustic guitar
Mandolin chop on beats 2 and 4
G, C, D, and A major keys dominate
Instrumental breaks traded between banjo, fiddle, and mandolin

progressions.name: Bluegrass I–IV–V
progressions.notes: Often played with quick bass runs between changes.

progressions.name: Circle fragment
progressions.notes: Jazzier than it sounds at tempo; common in gospel-tinged bluegrass.

progressions.name: Modal tag
progressions.notes: Mixolydian colour; the bVII adds a mountain-folk edge.

Bluegrass.rhythm: Fast 2/4 or cut-time feel; driving off-beat chop
Bluegrass.instruments: Banjo (5-string), mandolin, fiddle, acoustic guitar, upright bass

Blues.summary: African-American roots music built on the 12-bar form, bent notes, and call-and-response. Major and minor blues scales colour melodies over dominant 7th harmony.
tropes: 12-bar blues with I, IV, and V7 chords
Blue notes: b3, b5, b7 against major-key harmony
Shuffle or slow 4/4 groove
Question-and-answer phrasing between vocal and instrument

progressions.name: 12-bar quick change
progressions.numerals: I – IV – I – I | IV – IV – I – I | V – IV – I – V
progressions.notes: Quick change hits IV in bar 2; the most common blues form.

progressions.name: 8-bar blues
progressions.numerals: I – V | IV – V | I – V
progressions.example: C – G | F – G | C – G
progressions.notes: Shorter form; heard in early jazz-blues and some Chicago styles.

progressions.name: Minor blues
progressions.numerals: i – iv – i | iv – iv – i | V – iv – i – V
progressions.example: Am – Dm – Am | Dm – Dm – Am | E7 – Dm – Am – E7
progressions.notes: Natural minor or Dorian flavour; darker mood.

Blues.rhythm: Shuffle 12/8 or straight 4/4 with swung eighths
Blues.instruments: Electric guitar, harmonica, piano, drums, electric bass

Rock.summary: Blues and country DNA amplified and distorted. Power chords, strong riffs, and verse–chorus song structures define most subgenres from classic rock to punk.
tropes: Power chords (root + 5th) on guitar
Distortion, overdrive, and palm-muted riffs
I–bVII–IV (Mixolydian) and I–V–vi–IV progressions
Anthemic choruses with repeated hooks

progressions.name: Classic rock three-chord
progressions.notes: Louder, simpler, faster than the blues form it came from.

progressions.name: Mixolydian rock
progressions.notes: The bVII is a rock hallmark — think Allman Brothers, AC/DC.

progressions.name: Arena anthem
progressions.notes: Emotional minor start resolving to major; huge chorus energy.

Rock.rhythm: Straight 4/4; backbeat on 2 and 4; half-time for ballads
Rock.instruments: Electric guitar, bass, drums; keyboards in prog and arena rock

Jazz.summary: Harmony-forward music with extended chords, syncopation, and improvisation. Standards draw from show tunes, blues, and Latin rhythms.
tropes: ii–V–I cadences in major and minor
7th, 9th, 11th, and 13th chords
Swing feel and syncopated rhythms
Improvisation over chord changes (not just a single scale)

progressions.name: ii–V–I (major)
progressions.notes: The core jazz cadence; practice in every key.

progressions.name: Rhythm changes (A section)
progressions.notes: From Gershwin's I Got Rhythm; bebop proving ground.

progressions.name: Minor ii–V–I
progressions.notes: Half-diminished to altered dominant; darker jazz colour.

Jazz.rhythm: Swing 4/4; bossa nova and Latin subdivisions in other styles
Jazz.instruments: Piano, upright bass, drums, saxophone, trumpet, guitar

African.summary: A vast family of traditions — not one sound. West African highlife, East African benga, and Southern African mbira music share interlocking rhythms, cyclical forms, and call-and-response.
tropes: Interlocking rhythmic patterns (polyrhythm)
Pentatonic and hexatonic melodies
Cyclical song forms rather than Western verse–chorus
Call-and-response between lead vocal and chorus

progressions.name: Highlife loop
progressions.notes: Ghanaian highlife; horns and guitars layer over a steady groove.

progressions.name: Afrobeat ostinato
progressions.notes: Fela Kuti-style vamps; harmony stays put while rhythm section drives.

progressions.name: Pentatonic drone
progressions.notes: Melody moves on pentatonic degrees over a fixed tonic — common in mbira and griot traditions.

African.rhythm: Complex layered percussion; 12/8 bell patterns (son clave variants)
African.instruments: Kora, balafon, djembe, talking drum, electric guitar (in modern hybrids)

Mali Blues.summary: Desert blues from the Sahel — Ali Farka Touré, Tinariwen, and successors blend traditional Songhai and Tuareg music with electric guitar and blues-like phrasing.
tropes: Hypnotic modal vamps on one or two chords
Pentatonic melodies with blues-inflected bends
Assouf — longing, exile, and desert imagery in lyrics
Camel-gait rhythm feel (loping, steady pulse)

progressions.name: Desert vamp
progressions.notes: Tinariwen staple; guitar and vocals weave over the same two chords.

progressions.name: Ali Farka turn
progressions.notes: Simpler harmonic motion; the interest is in the picking pattern and timbre.

progressions.name: Modal drone
progressions.notes: Mixolydian minor flavour; open strings and ringing overtones.

Mali Blues.rhythm: Steady 4/4 or 6/8 lope; handclaps and percussion mark the pulse
Mali Blues.instruments: Electric guitar (often fingerpicked), calabash, tehardent (ngoni), handclaps

Folk.summary: Acoustic storytelling music passed through communities. Anglo-American folk favours open chords and diatonic melody; Celtic folk adds modes and dance rhythms.
tropes: Fingerpicked or strummed acoustic guitar
Diatonic melodies in major or modal minor
Narrative lyrics — work songs, protest, love ballads
Simple arrangements; voice is the focus

progressions.name: Campfire classic
progressions.notes: The same pop progression, played quietly on acoustic.

progressions.name: Celtic modal
progressions.notes: Dorian or Aeolian feel; no strong dominant pull.

progressions.name: American primitivism
progressions.notes: Carter Family and early country-folk; two-chord sections common.

Folk.rhythm: Straight or lightly swung; jig (6/8) and reel (4/4) in Celtic traditions
Folk.instruments: Acoustic guitar, banjo, mandolin, fiddle, voice
